stage-intentionally.label: Stage Intentionally
stage-intentionally.summary: Choose the exact files for the next commit and verify the staged snapshot before recording it.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.title: Stage One File, Not Everything
try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

`index.html` and `styles.css` both changed, but the next snapshot is only about navigation. Stage `index.html` and list the staged names.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: Path-specific staging selects one coherent change while leaving unrelated working-tree edits outside the next commit.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..

git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..

try_stage_intentionally_sketch0.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

`index.html` and `styles.css` both changed, but the next snapshot is only about navigation. Stage `index.html` and list the staged names.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

0.message: Do not use destructive history-rewriting commands in this course.
Run `git add index.html`, then check your answer again.

1.message: Run `git diff --staged --name-only`, then check your answer again.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.title: Inspect the Exact Staged Patch
try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

`index.html` is already staged while `styles.css` remains unstaged. Inspect the staged patch and then compare it with short status.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git diff --staged previews what the next commit would record, not every working-tree edit.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }

' > styles.css

try_stage_intentionally_sketch1.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

`index.html` is already staged while `styles.css` remains unstaged. Inspect the staged patch and then compare it with short status.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

0.message: Do not use destructive history-rewriting commands in this course.
Run `git diff --staged -- index.html`, then check your answer again.

1.message: Run `git status --short`, then check your answer again.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.title: Add a Second Related File
try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The staged navigation change also needs `nav.js`; `styles.css` is still unrelated. Stage `nav.js` and verify that exactly two files are staged.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: A focused snapshot may contain several files when they serve the same change.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- index.html

#!/usr/bin/env bash

rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site

git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'nav { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- index.html

try_stage_intentionally_sketch2.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The staged navigation change also needs `nav.js`; `styles.css` is still unrelated. Stage `nav.js` and verify that exactly two files are staged.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

0.message: Do not use destructive history-rewriting commands in this course.
Run `git add nav.js`, then check your answer again.

1.message: Run `git diff --staged --name-only`, then check your answer again.

try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.title: Verify the Boundary Before Commit
try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The navigation files are staged and the palette edit is not. Use staged and unstaged name-only views to prove the boundary.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_stage_intentionally_sketch3.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: Checking both sides of the index confirms what will be committed and what will remain for later.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

single-choice-1.title: Staging Area Purpose
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the staging area in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Consider how the staging area affects commits.

help.concept: The staging area allows you to prepare specific changes for a commit.
help.hint_1: The staging area helps manage what changes are included in a commit.
help.hint_2: It acts as a buffer between the working directory and the commit.

options.a: To prepare changes for a commit

multi-choice-1.title: Purpose of Staging
multi-choice-1.prompt: What is the purpose of staging in Git? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: The staging area selects exactly what the next commit will contain, so unrelated edits can remain outside the snapshot.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

options.a: To prepare specific changes for a commit

multi-choice-2.title: Inspecting Staged Changes
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which command allows you to inspect staged changes in Git?
multi-choice-2.hint: Think about the command that shows differences between staged changes and the last commit.

help.concept: Inspecting staged changes helps verify what will be committed.
help.hint_1: The command shows differences between staged changes and the last commit.
help.hint_2: It is similar to `git diff` but for staged changes.

drag-reorder-1.title: Staging Workflow Steps
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps in the correct order for staging and committing a file in Git.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with staging the file and end with committing it.

help.concept: Staging and committing involve specific steps in Git.
help.hint_1: Begin with adding the file to the staging area.
help.hint_2: End with committing the staged changes.

fill-blank-1.title: Command to Stage a File
fill-blank-1.prompt: Fill in the blank to stage a file named `index.html`.

help.concept: Staging a file prepares it for commit.
help.hint_2: Specify the file name after `git add`.

fill-blank-2.title: Unstaging a File
fill-blank-2.prompt: Fill in the blank to unstage a file named `index.html`.

help.concept: Unstaging a file moves it back to the working directory without losing changes.
help.hint_2: Specify the file name after `git restore --staged`.

stage-intentionally-sketch0.title: Build the Next Snapshot
stage-intentionally-sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
`git add` copies the current version of a path into the staging area. Stage the file that belongs to the next commit instead of staging everything by habit.

```bash
git add navigation.html
```

The working file remains editable. The staging area now holds a proposed snapshot of `navigation.html`.

Selective staging gives you control over commit boundaries, especially when several files changed for different reasons.

stage-intentionally-sketch1.title: Inspect What Is Staged
stage-intentionally-sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Use the staged diff to review the exact snapshot Git will commit:

```bash
git diff --staged
```

This view is different from plain `git diff`. The staged form compares the staging area with the latest commit; the plain form compares the working tree with the staging area.

A final staged review catches missing files and accidental additions before history is changed.

stage-intentionally-sketch2.title: Unstage Without Losing Work
stage-intentionally-sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Staging is reversible. To remove a file from the proposed snapshot while keeping its edits in the working tree, run:

```bash
git restore --staged navigation.html
```

The file returns to the unstaged section of status. Its contents are not discarded.

This command changes the staging decision, not the work itself—a useful distinction when reorganizing a commit.

stage-intentionally-sketch3.title: Keep One Purpose Together
stage-intentionally-sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
A strong commit groups files that serve one purpose. A navigation link and its matching style may belong together; an unrelated event correction probably does not.

Use status, `git diff`, and `git diff --staged` as three views of the same decision. The goal is not the smallest possible commit. The goal is a coherent snapshot another developer can understand later.
